quickId:
Single, hidden node on petiole
Even, brown-black body color
Crushed workers smell of rotten coconut
Trails along baseboards and counters
habitatNotes: No structural damage. Contaminates exposed food and forms persistent indoor trails, especially after rain pushes colonies inside.
management: Sanitation, moisture correction, and slow-acting sugar baits. Avoid repellent sprays that fragment colonies.

quickId:
Single petiole node, evenly rounded thorax
Workers vary widely in size within a colony
Coarse golden hairs on gaster
Sawdust-like frass below galleries
habitatNotes: Excavates galleries in moisture-damaged wood. Damage is structural over years, not days; finding frass is the typical first sign.
management: Locate and correct moisture sources. Trace foraging trails back to satellite nests; treat parent colony directly.

quickId:
Two petiole nodes
Parallel grooves on head and thorax
Soil mounds along sidewalks and slab edges
Slow, deliberate trails
habitatNotes: Nuisance only. Soil displacement under slabs occasionally noticeable.
management: Granular or gel baits placed near nest entrances. Seal slab cracks to limit indoor entry.

quickId:
Yellow to honey-amber body, darker gaster
Two petiole nodes, 12-segmented antennae with three-segmented club
Tiny — about 2 mm
Multiple satellite nests within heated structures
habitatNotes: Critical concern in hospitals and food-handling facilities. Sprays cause budding and worsen infestations.
management: Bait-only programs across the entire structure. Never use repellent contact insecticides.

quickId:
Two parallel dark stripes on pronotum
Light tan to caramel body
Nymphs nearly black with a pale stripe
Egg case carried by female until hatch
habitatNotes: Allergens and pathogen carriage. Feeds on virtually any organic residue.
management: Gel baits, IGRs, sanitation. Monitor with sticky traps in corners along walls.

quickId:
Reddish-brown with pale yellow pronotal margin
Largest common indoor roach
Adults fly short distances in warm weather
Associated with sewers, steam tunnels
habitatNotes: Indicator of plumbing or drainage issues. Strong musty odor in heavy infestations.
management: Seal floor drains and sewer access. Bait stations in utility chases.

quickId:
Glossy near-black body
Females wingless; males with short wings
Slow-moving compared to other species
Strong preference for damp, cool spaces
habitatNotes: Frequent in basements and crawlspaces; signals moisture problems.
management: Dehumidify, exclude. Granular baits in voids and along foundations.

quickId:
Apple-seed shape, flattened dorsally
Reddish-brown; engorged adults are darker and rounded
Banded segmentation on abdomen
Fecal spotting on seams and headboards
habitatNotes: Bite reactions vary widely. Not a confirmed disease vector under typical exposure.
management: Integrated approach: encasements, heat, vacuuming, targeted residuals. Inspect-confirm before treatment.

quickId:
Visually near-identical to bed bug
Longer fringe hairs on pronotum (microscope needed)
Found near roosts, not beds, in initial infestations
Bites people only after bats leave
habitatNotes: Indicator of a current or former bat colony in the structure.
management: Exclude bats first (legally and seasonally), then treat harborage. Treating without exclusion fails.

quickId:
Forewing two-toned: pale base, coppery outer two-thirds
Resting moths held tent-like over body
Larvae produce silk webbing in food
Often first noticed flying around lights
habitatNotes: Larvae contaminate grains, dried fruit, pet food, and birdseed with frass and webbing.
management: Find and discard the source — every package. Pheromone traps confirm eradication, not control.

quickId:
Six tooth-like projections on each side of pronotum
Slender, flattened body
Cannot fly indoors
Adults long-lived; populations build slowly
habitatNotes: Cannot attack whole sound kernels; thrives on broken grain, flour, cereal, dried fruit.
management: Discard infested product, vacuum shelves, use sealed containers.

quickId:
Bright red eyes
Tan to yellow-brown body
Slow, hovering flight near ripe fruit and drains
Larvae in fermenting organic film
habitatNotes: Indicator of fermenting matter — overripe fruit, beer/soda residue, organic film in drains.
management: Remove breeding material. Drain cleaning gels for biofilm. Apple-cider-vinegar traps as monitors.

quickId:
Heart-shaped, fuzzy wings held roof-like
Body and wings densely hairy ('moth fly')
Weak fluttering flight near drains and sumps
Larvae in drain biofilm
habitatNotes: No bite, no disease, but presence indicates standing biofilm.
management: Mechanical drain brushing followed by enzyme/bacterial drain gels. Chemical drain cleaners alone do not work.

quickId:
Six eyes in three pairs (most spiders have eight)
Violin-shaped marking on cephalothorax
Uniform tan legs without banding or spines
Rarely on webs in open view
habitatNotes: Bites are uncommon and require pressure against skin. Most suspected bites are misdiagnoses.
management: Glue boards along baseboards in undisturbed storage. Reduce clutter; shake out stored clothing.

quickId:
Glossy black with red dorsal spots and a split hourglass below
Small, irregular cobweb in protected outdoor sites
Females only — males are inconspicuous
Hangs upside-down in web
habitatNotes: Bites painful and medically significant; rarely fatal in healthy adults.
management: Exclusion, glove use in storage. Web removal disrupts harborage.

quickId:
Extremely long, thin legs (often confused with daddy long-legs)
Pale, almost translucent body
Loose, tangled cobweb in corners
Vibrates rapidly when disturbed
habitatNotes: Beneficial — captures other household spiders and insects.
management: Tolerate where possible. Web removal if appearance is a concern.

quickId:
Open, single-comb umbrella nest hung by a stalk
Slender, long-legged in flight; legs trail
Reddish-brown with yellow markings, variable
Calm unless nest is disturbed
habitatNotes: Stings painful but colonies small. Annual nests, abandoned each fall.
management: Only treat nests in high-contact areas. Evening application; freeze-style aerosols.

quickId:
Bright yellow and black banding, no body hair
Enclosed nest in ground cavity, wall void, or tree base
Aggressive defense within 3–4 m of nest
Late-summer scavenging at picnics
habitatNotes: Significant sting hazard; multiple stings possible. Allergic reactions are a real risk.
management: Mark entrance during daylight; treat at night. Professional removal for wall-void nests.

quickId:
Tapered, fish-shaped body with metallic silver scales
Three long tail filaments, two long antennae
Rapid, fish-like darting movement
Active at night in bathrooms, basements, attics
habitatNotes: Surface grazing on starches, sizing in book bindings, wallpaper paste, stored paper.
management: Reduce humidity below 50%. Sticky monitors confirm activity. Long-lived; control is gradual.

quickId:
Translucent pale-tan body, soft-bodied
About the size of a pinhead
Wingless indoor forms most common
Found on damp paper, behind wallpaper, in pantries
habitatNotes: Feeds on microscopic mold, not the paper itself. A moisture problem in disguise.
management: Dehumidify. The mold disappears, the booklice follow. No insecticide needed in most cases.
